revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/89919726|三方 (神道)]]"
revision.text:
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|三方に載せられた神饌（[[Katori Shrine|香取神宮]]）]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] '''三方'''（さんぼう）とは、[[Shinto|神道]]の神事において使われる、[[Shinsen|神饌]]を載せるための台である。古代には、高貴な人物に物を献上する際にも使用された。寺院でも同様のものが使われるが、この場合は[[Three Jewels|三宝]]（仏・法・僧）にかけて'''三宝'''（さんぽう）と書かれることもある。
== 構造 == 通常は[[Chamaecyparis obtusa|檜]]などの素木（しらき）による木製で、折敷（おしき）と呼ばれる盆の下に直方体状の台（胴）がついた形をしている。台の三方向に眼像(くりかた)と呼ばれる穴があいていることから、「三方」と呼ばれる。
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
台の穴の[[Design|意匠]]に決まりはないが、[[Giboshi|宝珠]]の形がよく用いられる。
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
There are also special shapes of mikata, such as ita-ashi mikata and maru-mikata.
また、四方に穴のあるタイプもあり、それを「四方」（しほう）と呼ぶ<ref name="#1">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref><ref name=":0" />。腹を切る際に短刀を載せるのは、三方ではなく四方である（[[Seppuku|切腹#作法]]）。
The sambo used at shrines include tozan sambo, nuri-zambo, shiroki sambo, shikaku sambo, round sambo, china sambo, ashi-uchi sambo, and nagasamikata.<ref>『神祭具便覧４０巻』民俗工芸平成２８年９月発行全４３８頁１７９頁</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 高杯（盃、坏） * 菓子器 * 供笥（供華） * 霊供膳（仏膳） * 仏飯器（仏器） * 膳、箱膳 * 神具 * 仏具 [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

revision.text:
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] ''''Sanbo'''' is a stand used in the Shinto rituals of [[Shinto|shinto]] to place [[Shinsen|shinten]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] (Buddha, Dharma, and priest).
== 構造 == 通常は[[Chamaecyparis obtusa|檜]]などの素木（しらき）による木製で、折敷（おしき）と呼ばれる盆の下に直方体状の台（胴）がついた形をしている。台の三方向に眼像(くりかた)と呼ばれる穴があいていることから、「三方」と呼ばれる。
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
台の穴の[[Design|意匠]]に決まりはないが、[[Giboshi|宝珠]]の形がよく用いられる。
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
There are also special shapes of mikata, such as ita-ashi mikata and maru-mikata.
また、四方に穴のあるタイプもあり、それを「四方」（しほう）と呼ぶ<ref name="#1">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref><ref name=":0" />。腹を切る際に短刀を載せるのは、三方ではなく四方である（[[Seppuku|切腹#作法]]）。
The sambo used at shrines include tozan sambo, nuri-zambo, shiroki sambo, shikaku sambo, round sambo, china sambo, ashi-uchi sambo, and nagasamikata.<ref>『神祭具便覧４０巻』民俗工芸平成２８年９月発行全４３８頁１７９頁</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 高杯（盃、坏） * 菓子器 * 供笥（供華） * 霊供膳（仏膳） * 仏飯器（仏器） * 膳、箱膳 * 神具 * 仏具 [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] ''''Sanbo'''' is a stand used in the Shinto rituals of [[Shinto|shinto]] to place [[Shinsen|shinten]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] (Buddha, Dharma, and priest).
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[Design|design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[Giboshi|pearl]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
There are also special shapes of mikata, such as ita-ashi mikata and maru-mikata.
There is also a type with holes on all four sides, which is called "shiho" (four sides)<ref name="#1">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref><ref name=":0" />。When cutting the belly, the dagger is placed on four sides of the body, not three （[[Seppuku]]）。
The sambo used at shrines include tozan sambo, nuri-zambo, shiroki sambo, shikaku sambo, round sambo, china sambo, ashi-uchi sambo, and nagasamikata.<ref>『神祭具便覧４０巻』民俗工芸平成２８年９月発行全４３８頁１７９頁</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 ==
* 高杯（盃、坏） * 菓子器 * 供笥（供華） * 霊供膳（仏膳） * 仏飯器（仏器） * 膳、箱膳 * 神具 * 仏具 [[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] '{{Nihongo|Sanbo|三方|Sanbō}}'''<nowiki/>'''' is a stand used in the Shinto rituals of [[Shinto|shinto]] to place [[Shinsen|shinten]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>

The sambo used at shrines include tozan sambo, nuri-zambo, shiroki sambo, shikaku sambo, round sambo, china sambo, ashi-uchi sambo, and nagasamikata.<ref>『神祭具便覧４０巻』民俗工芸平成２８年９月発行全４３８頁１７９頁</ref>。

{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] '{{Nihongo|Sanbo|三方|Sanbō}}' is a stand used in the Shinto rituals of [[shinto]] to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|Cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
[[Category:Japanese culture]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[en:Sanbo]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{Shinto shrines}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Culture of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhist ritual implements]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto religious objects]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sanbo]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Sanbo]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Sanbo]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[en:Sanbo]]

The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Shrine|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}

Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[Three Jewels|Three Treasures]] ([[Sangha]], [[Dharma]], [[Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

There are no rules for the [[:en:Design|Design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Jingu|Katori Jingu]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for [[:en:Refuge in Buddhism|Three Treasures]] ([[:en:Sangha|Sangha]], [[:en:Dharma|Dharma]], [[:en:The Buddha|The Buddha]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the [[:en:Design|Design]] of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for {{ill|Refuge in Buddhism|en|Refuge in Buddhism|WD=Q228220|lt=Three Treasures}} ({{ill|Sangha|en|Sangha|WD=Q193944|lt=Sangha}}, {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma}}, {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha}}).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the {{ill|Design|en|Design|WD=Q82604|lt=Design}} of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sanbo]][[ja:三方 (神道)]][[th:ซัมโบ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sanbo]][[ja:三方 (神道)]][[th:ซัมโบ]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Sanbo]][[ja:三方 (神道)]][[th:ซัมโบ]]
{{Short description|Shinto altar implement}}
[[File:Votive_offering_of_the_Shinto,Katori-jingu,Katori-city,Japan.JPG|thumb|Sacrificial offering placed on three sides ([[Katori Jingū|Katori Jingū]])]] [[File:MET_DP135911.jpg|thumb|Sanpo with pine tree decorations and lobsters for New Year's Day. Painted by Shuntei Katsukawa, circa 1815.]] {{Nihongo|'''Sanbo'''|三方|Sanbō}} is a stand used in Shinto rituals to place [[shinsen]]. In ancient times, it was also used to present objects to a noble person. The same type of stand is also used in temples, but in this case it is sometimes written '''sampo''', which stands for {{ill|Refuge in Buddhism|en|Refuge in Buddhism|WD=Q228220|lt=Three Treasures|1=Refuge in Buddhism}} ({{ill|Sangha|en|Sangha|WD=Q193944|lt=Sangha|1=Sangha}}, {{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|WD=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}, {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|WD=Q9441|lt=The Buddha|1=The Buddha}}).<ref>{{Cite web |last=第２版,世界大百科事典内言及 |first=精選版 日本国語大辞典,ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典,食器・調理器具がわかる辞典,デジタル大辞泉,百科事典マイペディア,事典 日本の地域ブランド・名産品,葬儀辞典,世界大百科事典 |title=三方とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%89%E6%96%B9-71456 |access-date=2022-06-10 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}}</ref>
== Structure == Usually made of [[Chamaecyparis obtusa|cypress]] or other wood, it has a rectangular base (body) attached to a tray called an oshiki. It is called "sanpō" (three-way) because there are holes called kurikata in three directions on the base.
Originally, the ori-shiki and the stand were separated and the ori-shiki was placed on the stand when used, and sometimes the ori-shiki was used alone without the stand. Today, the folded mat and stand are completely combined, and those used only with the folded mat are prepared separately from the three sides of the mat.
There are no rules for the {{ill|Design|en|Design|WD=Q82604|lt=Design|1=Design}} of the holes in the stand, but the [[giboshi]] shape is often used.
The folded cloth has a binding to hold the edge board in place, which is made so that the side without holes is the opposite side. When offering to the gods, the side without the hole (the side opposite the binding) should face the gods. When holding the three sides of the bowl with the food offerings on it, the thumbs should be placed on the right and left edges, and the other fingers should be placed on the orihiki and the stand, holding the bowl at eye level. However, in the court etiquette, the fingers are traditionally placed inside the oriziki.<ref name=":0">『出雲大社教布教師養成講習会』発行出雲大社教教務本庁</ref>
